customer_name: Out of Africa Wildlife Park, LLC
customer_addr:
4020 N. Cherry Rd
Camp Verde, AZ 86322
site_name: Out of Africa Wildlife Park, LLC

citations.narrative:
There are multiple low areas of the post-and-wire (6-inch by 6-inch panel fencing) public barriers separating the public
from dangerous animals' primary enclosures where a guest may easily step over the barrier to access the primary
enclosure directly. The low spots appear to have occurred due to weathering, damage and/or human action. For example,
approximately 10 feet of public barrier in front of the primary enclosure for a leopard “Enoch” is 26 inches in height.
Multiple big cat enclosures have a break in the public barrier fencing created by the presence of a smooth metal bar
intended for keepers to step over in order to access the enclosure for feeding, training and enrichment of animals. For
example, there is an 8-foot smooth metal bar that is part of the public barrier in front of “Kora” the lioness that is 26 inches
in height from the ground. There are approximately a dozen primary enclosures housing lions, leopards, tigers, bears,
mountain lions and a hyena with low spots in the public barrier. At Critter Court, a picnic table is placed 2 feet from the
smooth bar top section (measures 28 inches high) of a public barrier surrounding a tiger enclosure. A member of the
public could feasibly use the seat and top of the picnic table as steps to breach the public barrier.
Failure to provide sufficient barriers between the public and animals such as wild canids and wild felids does not minimize
the risk of harm to the public or the animals. During public exhibition, there must be sufficient distance and/or barriers
between the animal and the general viewing public so as to assure the safety of the animals and the public.
Correct by 02 June 2026.

At the time of public interactions during the giraffe feeding encounter on the Safari tour, there was a lack of adequate
attendants and more than minimal risk of harm as follows:
•The driver, the only attendant for the group of about 20 adults and children, was observed standing at the front of the bus
when stopped for the entirety of the giraffe feeding encounter. The employee could not readily and easily monitor public
contact with the giraffes occurring at the back of the bus, nor could the employee intervene if necessary, at the back of the
bus. The bus is a standard school bus length (approximately 35 feet) that has been retrofitted to have open air sides with
intermittent metal supports connected to a roof. A method of feeding publicly allowed was for people to hold the
approximately 4-inch-long acacia twig in between their lips for the giraffes to take, which occurred at the back of the bus.
•The sole driver/attendant was physically blocked from the public when both giraffes placed their whole heads and
multiple feet of neck into the bus interior at the same time.
•A giraffe was observed trying to stick their head into a small opening measuring 20 inches by 20 inches on the side of the
bus located at the approximate middle of the vehicle.
Lack of employee oversight of contact between the animals and the public may lead to injury to the patrons and/or injury
or distress to the animals. A responsible, knowledgeable, and readily identifiable employee or attendant must be present
at all times during periods of public contact.
Correct by 02 March 2026.

There is excessive rust coating the metal mesh walls in the sheltered portion of the enclosure for two adult brown lemurs.
The animals can come into daily contact with these surfaces. Exposure to rust may cause veterinary care issues in
animals. Any surfaces that come in contact with nonhuman primates must be free of excessive rust that prevents the
required cleaning and sanitization, or affects the structural strength of the surface.
Correct by 06 April 2026.

Sharp points are present in the enclosure housing three adult ring-tailed lemurs by the Serengeti Overlook Trail. Two
sharp metal screws approximately two inches in length each are protruding from a branch. Sharp metal points that may
come in contact with nonhuman primates can cause injuries. All surfaces must be maintained to be free of jagged edges
or sharp points that might injure the animals.
Correct by 09 February 2026.

The primary enclosure housing two Patagonian maras, “Bean” and “Sprout”, has walls 4 feet in height with an open-air
top. During the “Creature Feature” show with one of the mara, the employee stated that the species can jump 6 feet high
and the mara at the facility have been observed doing so. Housing high jumping animals in enclosures with insufficient
wall height or lack of other means to reasonably contain the animals may allow them to escape or become injured. The
indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and constructed as appropriate for the species. They shall
be maintained in good repair to protect the animals from injury and to contain the animals.
Correct by 06 April 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Jessica Siegal-Willott n

Handling of animals.

species.common: GRAY WOLF / GREY WOLF / TIMBER WOLF
